content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Terry Gilliam-esq dystopian dark comedies. ‘It’s the future but nothing works and life is comedically grim’ vibe.

**Score:** 47
**Body:** Brazil and 12 Monkeys are some of my favorite movies and want something that captures that weird ‘there is futuristic technology but it all sucks and everyone is miserable and apathetic’ in a funny way feeling.

Richard Ayoade’s **The Double, Idiocracy, and Delicatessen** are the closest thing I’ve seen that matches this vibe, also some of my favorites. Any other recommendations?

Hitchhiker’s Guide and that Sam Rockwell movie from this year also kind of fit that vibe but not quite what I’m looking for. Futurama too. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. City of Lost Children has much more grim sci-fi dystopia vibes than Delicatessan.

Have you seen The Zero Theorem? Not my favorite Gilliam but it's definitely in the vein you are describing. 


  - Sorry to Bother You

The Lobster

Isle of Dogs

A Scanner Darkly
  - Came here to suggest City of Lost Children. 
2. Cosmopolis


Dark City
  - I am #1 Dark City glazer that movie is so incredible and deserving of a high budget remake 
3. Tank Girl.

Edit: If you like Sam Rockwell you should watch Moon. Not haha funny, but grim future vibe is there.

The Fifth Element fits the technology doesn't make things better vibe too. And very funny.
4. Don't Look Up, and Mickey 17 are both the same vibe as idiocracy.

Blade runner, and Cyberpunk Edgerunners will hit the vibe of nothing works in a shit society controlled by evil corporations.

Demolition Man hits that vibe where the cops are so reliant on tech that they don't know how to function.

As far as evil governments in control, Andor is amazing. So is The movie Equilibrium. Dark City as well.

You might also like Repo The Genetic Opera, a musical where the company tears out your organs if you cant pay rent on your liver.

Fallout TV series. weird tech, absurd living conditions and comedically grim
5. If you haven’t watched *Good Luck, Have Fun, Don’t Die* I HIGHLY recommend it, very much 12 Monkeys vibes, a bit brighter and not exactly junky tech but so so good and Sam Rockwell. 
6. The Fifth Element
7. Trouble in Mind

Dead End Drive-In

Death Race 2000

Prayer of the Rollerboys

Solarbabies

Crime Zone

Class of 1999

Demolition Man

Escape from New York /L.A.

The Running Man (87)

Total Recall (90)

## 2. Movies that will inspire one to stop brainrot/bedrot lifestyle, and start actually living?

**Score:** 45
**Body:** I find myself caught in quick-media meaningless consumption spirals, I'm trying to find some movies that could pull me out of this state. 

Doesn't have to be "happy" or 'inspirational" in a traditional sense, just something to shake up my mind

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Last Holiday starring Queen Latifa is excellent. She’s diagnosed with a terminal illness and only has a few weeks to live. She goes on a trip that she’s been dreaming of her entire life and suddenly it feels like the universe is conspiring in her favor. It’s inspiring and uplifting. Highly suggest it. 
  - Oh lord! It’s so refreshing to see this comment! I have loved that movie  as long as I can remember! Brilliant movie! 
2. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty 
3. Yes man (2008)
4. Joe vs. the Volcano
5. Good Will Hunting – get on with life and don’t stay in the comfortable just because it is comfortable. 

Karate Kid – the original


6. I would recommend older movies as they are slower paced and void of CGI/big budget tricks. Cool Hand Luke and 12 Angry Men are a couple good ones.
7. Everything Everywhere All at Once
8. *Amélie*
9. - I Saw the TV glow (2024) is what helped me but if you have a shortened attention span then I suggest:
- Kajillionaire (2020) - its meaningful media and definitely about shaking up a toxic but comfortable routine. It definitely shakes up the mind

## 3. Best movies featuring the ocean in some way

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I’d love to get a list of movies featuring - at least indirectly - the ocean. From The Old Man and the Sea to The Abyss to Sphere to Thunderball (really!) the ocean is a prominent part of some of my favorites.

Bonus points for classic movies, Atlantis or lost continent features, or aliens!

Thank you! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Master and commander 
2. Deeply underrated & underseen — Robert Redford’s *All Is Lost* (2013)
3. Since no one has mentioned it yet let's go with a simple classic.

***Cast Away with Tom Hanks.***

Definitely one of my favorites.

Worth a watch if you've never seen it before.
4. Jaws
5. Waterworld
6. Ponyo

The life of Pi

Ghost Ship - it has one of the best opening scenes in a horror/suspense movie 
7. The Perfect Storm
8. Joe Versus the Volcano.  
  
Captain Ron.  
9. The Hunt for Red October
10. Overboard

## 4. Movies with the biggest plot twists?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Okay, so I have seen several movies that were said to have the biggest plot twists in movie history, but nothing really surprised me. Everytime I finish a movie that was supposed to shock me, I'm just like "this was it?". Maybe it's because while watching a film, my mind keeps trying to guess what the twist might be, I think of every possible solution and I usually end up guessing it. Therefore, I am looking for movies where the twist is so unexpected that I won't be able guess it and in the end, I will be so shocked that I will think about the movie for days. The film doesn't have to be in English, I am open to anything.



Movies I have already watched:

Vertigo, Gaslight, The Orphan (these three actually surprised me but I watched them when I was about 12 lol)

The invisible guest (this one surprised me, I actually liked it the most from the ones on the list)

The Handmaiden, Shutter Island, Memento, Perfect blue, Mulholland Drive (these ones surprised me a bit too)

Fight Club (surprised me a bit, but feels a bit overhyped)

The Sixth Sense

The Others

Chinatown

Prisoners

The Scream movies

The Game

Goodnight Mommy (knew what the twist was gonna be about halfway into the film)

The Skin I Live In

Identity (this one surprised me too but I didn't like the movie that much)

The Prestige (I couldn't guess the twist, but I wasn't too shocked by it either)

Movies I haven't seen because they have been spoiled by the internet: Gone Girl, The Usual Suspects, Primal Fear, Inception, Oldboy, Se7en, Saw

  
Thank you if you have read it all and I'm really looking forward to see your recommendations! 😄 (btw sorry about my grammar, English is not my native language)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. honestly if you go into every movie trying to "beat" it and guess the ending, you’re kind of ruining it for yourself. just vibe for once.


2. The Secret in their Eyes (2009). Make sure to watch the original one.

Incendies

Spoorlos
3. The Village
4. The Sixth Sense. 

Turns out that guy in the hair piece is Bruce Willis the whole time. 
5. Arrival!!! My favorite 
6. Frailty
7. Arlington Road (1999)

Parasite (2019)

Saw (2004)
8. Not a plot twist exactly, but the movie ends in an atypical way for its genre and the time in which it was made.
Last American Virgin (1982).




High Tension (2003) had a twist that most didn't see coming, but many argue it's more of a plot hole than a clever twist. New French Extremity was still pretty new back then, so i really enjoyed it, irrespective of the debate over the "twist". But, I think if you go into into looking to guess the twist, you probably won't enjoy the movie overall. 
In my experience, films are always more enjoyable if you let go of the wheel and let yourself just go along for the ride. It's not about shutting off your brain so much as it is forcing your brain to stay fully present in the moment. 
9. Predestination with Ethan hawke and Sarah Snook
10. The Sting

## 5. Movies like Waiting… (2005)

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I just watched Waiting… for the first time in years and remembered how much I loved it. Can you recommend movies like it? Young people at work figuring their lives out with lots of raunchy, funny stuff happening. That sounds like Clerks, but I’ve seen that a million times. Thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Adventureland 
2. Employee of the Month 
  - This movie isn’t talked about enough!     
Dane and Dax at their funniest and Jessica holds her own with them.  
3. You would probably like the show Party Down 
4. Not work themed but similar era, Eurotrip. 
5. Empire Records!
6. The Slammin' Salmon (2009) might fit, is more raunchy humor with a late 20s? person working in a restaurant figuring out the type of person they want to be rather than figuring their whole life out
7. Out Cold (2001)
8. Office Space is a good work comedy with kind of off-beat and raunchy humor, although I think the ages of the main characters skews a little higher. 

Friday takes an almost opposite premise with the whole thing taking place the day after the main character gets fired. 

You might also like Everybody Wants Some by Richard Linklater which sometimes feels like a spiritual successor to his movie Dazed and Confused and focuses on College Baseball players enjoying the final days of their summer before school begins and responsibilities settle in. 
9. 40 year old virgin

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What’s a movie death that absolutely shocked you?

**Score:** 2185
**Body:** What’s a movie scene where a character dies unexpectedly and you’re caught off guard?

For me it’s the >!elevator scene in The Departed where DiCaprio gets shot.!< I was totally caught off guard and stunned into silence.

I literally shouted “WHAT?!?” When I first watched it.

What’s that one scene for you?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ahhhh man I just shot Marvin in the face. 
  - “WHY DID YOU DO THAT?!?!?”

Fantastic choice! That entire sequence is crazy good.
2. Julianne Moore in Children of Men
  - That’s a perfect choice. That car chase sequence is just breathtaking.
3. Llewellyn Moss
4. A lot of people will say Deep Blue Sea. That scene. For me though the one you mention is top of my list. I absolutely shouted the same thing!
5. I won't be reading this thread because spoilers.



Having said that, Brad Pitt in Burn after reading.
6. Vince Vega on the toilet
7. It’s been said a lot, but for good reason. Drew Barrymore, in SCREAM. It’s been talked about so much at this point. But that’s because those of us that saw it when it came out, we were absolutely stunned when it happened. We all went in thinking she was going to be Final Girl, or at least have a big third act casualty. It was wild to be a teen when that original trilogy came out.
8. Steve Coogan character in Tropic Thunder 

## 2. A pretty entertaining satire carried by the great Ralph Fiennes. What are your thoughts on The Menu?

**Score:** 1504

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "You went to college??" "Yes." "Which one??" "Brown." "Student loans??" "No??" "I'm sorry, you're dying."

Excellent.

(Edit: my fault about the original misquote)
  - I have some sympathy for the characters of John Leguizamo and assistant. Unlike the other patrons they are at least aware of being not great people, are trying to be reasonable, and are not hurtful towards others (well, unless you really count that shitty movie he stared in…). Out of all the patrons they least deserved their fate. 
    - You don't understand. He ruined Chef's only day off in like a month. Absolutely RUINED.
    - The issue Slowik had with the Actor was more nuanced than most of the others.  Slowik hated everyone in that room for compromising art with money and corrupting him.  The Actor was part of that same corrupted system - he did the movie for the paycheck, not because he valued the art being made.  This offended Slowik because it was a reflection of his own corruption.  The Actor was just another tumor that needed to be cut out, like everyone in the room and even Slowik himself.
2. I just saw it and I loved it. I didn't know what to expect. Ralph Fiennes is brilliant and I've got a lot of appreciation for Anna Taylor Joy. Nice to see John Leguizamo as well. 
  - Nicholas Holt always does a great job too.
3. Fun Dark Comedy and Ralph Fiennes never misses.
4. This movie altered my brain chemistry and permanently changed the way I say “Tortillas”
5. The face and reaction he gave when she told him that she wanted a cheeseburger for dinner always gets me. It's the delivery on how he gave that burger from start to finish was just so satisfying. Like he waited for years for someone to remind him where he came from. Like, I know that was the entire point, but it's the way Ralph Fiennes responds "I can make a cheeseburger." 
  - Fiennes delivered the real life version of this reaction. I think of the critic in Ratatouille when I watch The Menu all the time 🤣

![gif](giphy|eZjrixTYeiCT6)

## 3. Are these the new modern horror icons?

**Score:** 1384

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’d remove Rachel McAdams. Her role in Send Help wasn’t nearly as impactful as Aunt Gladys or any of the other characters on there
  - Also... I don't really believe the horror tag is even accurate in the context of the movie. I *guess* but it felt like a stretch outside of just having some blood and guts.
    - Yeah, aside from it being a Raimi movie, it's not really a horror movie. My wife hates horror, but she suggested we see Send Help and she loved it.
      - Yeah it’s more survival-thriller with classic Raimi touches and jump scares
  - Should’ve put the lady from Bring Her Back instead.
2. It would be helpful if you posted where these shots are from. I don’t recognize the bottom four.
  - It, Terrifier, Pearl, Send Help, Longlegs, Weapons, Smile, Sinners, Evil Dead Rise and Barbarian
  - Which is an answer in itself...  
  
There are good movies here, but I wouldn't describe any of the characters as “icons,” with the exception of Pennywise—though that's a special case, since the previous version was already hugely popular.
3. It's been a horror icon since the 80s, out of who is on screen, Art the clown is the only one that has the same visual iconography of something like Jason or Freddy.

Although i do see a lot of Maxxxine/pearl memes.
4. No Toni Collette is insane. 

## 4. We lost Donald Gibb, one of the best tough guys from the 80's. RIP.

**Score:** 1365

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|ToMjGpBXNcSXyqLurAs)


2. ![gif](giphy|A9KfKenpqNDfa)
3. I just watched Bloodsport last weekend. It made me really miss the buddy trope of 80s action movies. 

RIP to another great memory maker from my childhood.
4. **OGRE!**
  - “What if C-A-T really spelled dog?”
5. ![gif](giphy|XuzxKLeDVXzzy)
6. ![gif](giphy|5abjXZcMmGvRu)
7. I was a host then a server at Chili’s in Torrance, CA in the late 90’s, he would come in with his family all the time. He was such a cool, and friendly dude. Got to be his server a couple times, never brought up “Ogre” or “Jackson” but he could tell I knew who he was but I felt like he was there to enjoy dinner with his family so, I just would shoot the shit with him about whatever game was on or how life was going in general. He was always super chill and just a humble and nice guy. Good tipper, didn’t make a big deal of who he was, just liked to blend in and eat at Chili’s of all places. If it was just him and his wife they would sit at booths in the bar area and watch games, if the kids were with them they would sit at a 6 top in the same section usually where you could still see the tv’s in the bar area lol.
8. in Bloodsport it seems his character is killed in the ring causing the main character played by Jean-Claude Van Damme to swear revenge on his lost buddy. A few scenes later he is drinking beer out of a can in the hospital. Movie gold
  - https://preview.redd.it/3g6y3tdbbw0h1.png?width=402&format=png&auto=webp&s=cbdc4cbcbfd85105059201c966b99f5492e20d3a



## 5. Just finished watching Predator: Badlands. I thought it was very entertaining!

**Score:** 943

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sometimes a movie doesn’t need to reinvent cinema to be a genuinely entertaining watch
  - Between Prey and now Predator: Badlands, I feel like they're doing pretty well with this new direction in the Predator franchise.
  - Agreed. Just like chefs don't need to reinvent dishes, they just need to do a really good job preparing it.

Novelty is good.

Novelty isn't the *only* good.
2. I really enjoyed it also! I loved how the Predator communicated for the first time in this movie. Also liked the partnership. 
  - I liked that they finally gave the Predator more personality instead of just making it a silent killing machine again


  - Buddy movies where the characters are really different work for a reason. I loved it so much. Honestly, I wouldn’t have believed this was a real idea before it came out. They took a big chance and it was amazing. 
3. It is definitely entertaining. I did enjoy Prey more though. 
4. Definitely a solid Predator film.

I put it up there with Predators.
5. Watched it last night also. I was going in with a LOT lower expectations so a solid surprise there. Interested to see the animation after this one.
6. It’s Predator as a buddy comedy.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Can we criticize Nolan’s writing without being called a hater?

**Score:** 124
**Body:** I genuinely don’t understand why criticism of Christopher Nolan is treated differently from criticism of every other filmmaker. People constantly analyze and criticize other directors for weak dialogue, story issues, screenplay flaws, historical/mythological inaccuracies, or characterization, but when it comes to Nolan, a lot of fans instantly jump to defend everything.

The response is usually the same: “he uses IMAX,” “he uses practical effects,” “his movies are technical masterpieces,” etc. But great cinematography and practical filmmaking shouldn’t automatically make a movie immune to criticism.

Even in The Odyssey trailer, lines like Tom Holland casually saying “dad” felt oddly modern for a mythological epic, yet many people dismiss even small criticisms like that as “hate.”

Why can’t Nolan’s movies be discussed critically the same way we discuss every other director’s films?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It seems like they can, as I have only seen criticism about this film, literally not one post about someone excited to see it. Why do you feel like criticism of his films isn't allowed?
  - I refuse to believe this comment lives in my reality. The reality I live in is the complete opposite of this.
  - Here’s me saying I’m excited 
2. IHe has a lot of fans for sure, but I don’t really see this. I’m not a Nolan fan, but I never feel like I’m alone in that. Anytime I post my criticism of him there is a good amount of agreement.
  - Im a Nolan fan but there is plenty to criticise. I started to see a pattern emerging during Dark Knight Rises that I have noticed in other films now.

Still love his work, but as with most creatives I worry he gets surrounded by too many yes men.
    - The Dark Knight Rises is an interesting example people often site. There are obviously a lot of issues with the writing in that movie in just about every regard. Why I think it is an interesting example is that literally none of those issues started in TDKR, they have pretty much always been present in Nolan's writing. I see the primary difference is that in his pervious films he either papered over the cracks better or people just weren't aware enough of them to notice.  
 
Everything people complain about being wrong the TDKR was also in TDK, but TDK had Heath Ledger giving an all time great performance to distract from how bad the writing was.
      - one of my favorite Nolan films is both the most and the least nolan at the same time. At the time of its release I thought he must have been working on it for decades because it didn't have the flaws that I was used to when it came to Nolan. Other Nolan films came out and that jarringly cold staple of his writing returned. I think I finally saw the BTS for inception last year, and it was then that I found out Leo basically hated the script, said it on camera, became an uncredited writer to which Nolan thanked him for changing the emotional integrity of the film.
      - For me it was more so that I was old enough to see the problem. I was much younger for TDK and as a result didnt notice the issues as strongly. 
3. Seems odd to want to critique the writing of a film that hasn’t even been released yet, but you can do whatever you want.

I find it funny that people act like they can’t criticize Nolan, when there are far more critical Nolan posts than posts lauding him. 

“Why can’t Nolan’s movies be discussed critically the same way we discuss every other director’s films?”

Who says they can’t? Posts in this sub generally get similar effort back to what was put in. This is a post criticizing people who criticize criticism. What do you want us to debate here that is of any value?

4. I’m ambivalent on Nolan, and have had mixed at best feelings about quite a few of his movies, but I have never found people overwhelmingly hostile to any criticism of him on Reddit. 

The director I dislike for whom people seem to get the most emotional and defensive about the slightest criticism isn’t Nolan; it’s Wes Anderson. 


## 2. Blue Heron (2026)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** "A family of six settles into their new home on Vancouver Island as internal dynamics are slowly revealed through the eyes of the youngest child."

I watched this film recently and was struck by it’s tenderness, great direction and the clever way in which it unfolds . What a beautiful film. It’s pretty difficult to write about without spoilers too, but it's a story about family and difficulty. Shades of Eliza Hitman style neo-realism at some points. The first half an hour is quite slow paced and considering I went in blind to this film, I actually had  a few doubts about where it was all leading to. We see the family go about their business trying to fit in and struggling to really balance everything healthily as there is a fair amount of internal disruption.

After the opening act, lines between fiction and film-making start to blur and wobble and devastate. It will take me a while to process but what an amazing job Sophy Romvari did at directing this. One of the best films I've seen for a long while. If you like the films of Christian Petzold, Mia Hansen-Løve, Eliza Hittman, Joachim Trier, this might be for you.

Genuinely brilliant film. Has anybody else seen this one?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. So... it's not about a blue heron

Because I like herons :'c


  - I'm sorry, is this a Simpson's joke you're repeating? I dont understand.
    - The first part is just a throwaway comment, it got removed for not meeting length requirements so I added the rest. It's from a S11 episode where Homer becomes a food critic but he can never fill the word count so he just pads out his submissions with repetitions of 'screw flanders'
      - Ah, I see. Thanks for the clarification!

## 3. Goodbye to Language in 3D on a projector

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Does anyone know if there are ways that one could watch *Goodbye to Language* in 3D without a 3D blu-ray or projector (for instance, I have access to a relatively powerful projector but am unsure how a 3D copy of the blu-ray might look with that). Or perhaps have there been any versions of the film produced that utilize a cruder blue-red type of 3D. 

I will be watching this on a lecture hall projector. 

If it's just a lost cause, 2D will do. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You’ll only be able to watch in 3D with the 3D blu ray and a compatible player. Your projector may be compatible, but you’d need to share what model it is to know for sure, or you could check the manufacturer’s instructions. Some of the more technically-oriented film subreddits may be a better bet for this kind of question?
2. just googled "how to watch goodbye to language in 3d" and the very first top link was to some red-blue version. Not sure if I should link to that here, but it exists!


Yeah, for the proper experience you propably need the proper gear, bluray and tv/projecter both.
3. I watched it on my VR headset. 

If you don't have access to one, a used Quest2 is fairly inexpensive nowadays. I see them for around $60 to $70 and they eliminate many of the problem of watching 3D films. If you cannot watch this film in 3D then you shouldn't really bother as it's most of the experience. That being said it is a very innovative film and there needs to be more stereoscopic experiments like this. 

You can also find a 3d blue ray and convert the film with one of many apps to anaglyph blue red 3d but it's not the best viewing experience but you will get the effect.

4. I've pirated 3d movies in the past, you can cross your eyes and watch it in 3d on any display lmaoo

Its basically 2 movies playing side by side one for each eye and then you cross your eyes until you have one on top of the other and it's in focus

## 4. Thoughts on Paul Mazursky?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Someone recently started a thread on Mazursky on r/blankies and I thought I'd ask about him here, because his filmography is a real blind spot for me. I've only seen one of his movies, Bob & Carol & Ted & Alice, and I didn't love it.

On the surface, it's odd that a New Hollywood filmmaker with five Oscar nominations has been so forgotten in film circles. Especially when he has some other claims to fame: working with a young Stanley Kubrick in the early 50s, helping create The Monkees, acting in The Sopranos and Curb Your Enthusiasm. 

Any thoughts on Mazursky? His body of work? Why he's slipped into obscurity? 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorite directors. Paul Mazursky had one of the great humanist sensibilities in American cinema. His films feel conversational in the best sense — messy, funny, neurotic, wounded, affectionate. He understood modern adults: people who intellectualize their feelings, sabotage their happiness, chase reinvention, and still somehow remain lovable.

What makes Mazursky special is that he never treats emotional confusion as something to “solve.” Instead, he observes it with warmth and curiosity. His characters talk too much, desire too much, flee too much — and he lets all of that breathe onscreen. There’s an improvisational looseness in his films that makes them feel alive rather than engineered.

His run through the 1970s and early ’80s is remarkable:
Bob & Carol & Ted & Alice captured the anxieties and absurdities of sexual liberation with uncanny timing.
An Unmarried Woman gave one of the era’s most intelligent portraits of divorce and female self-definition.
Moscow on the Hudson balanced immigrant melancholy and American optimism with rare generosity.
Harry and Tonto turned aging and loneliness into something tender and expansive rather than sentimental.

But Tempest is perhaps his most hauntingly beautiful work.
Mazursky takes Shakespeare’s The Tempest and transforms it into a deeply modern emotional exile story. Instead of theatrical grandeur, he gives us emotional weather: middle-aged collapse, erotic confusion, spiritual exhaustion, the fantasy of escape. The Greek island setting becomes almost mythic — sun-bleached and dreamlike — yet the emotions remain painfully recognizable.
The performances are extraordinary:

John Cassavetes brings raw volatility and vulnerability.
Gena Rowlands carries immense emotional intelligence in every scene.
Susan Sarandon radiates freedom and danger simultaneously.
And Raul Julia practically dances through the movie with ecstatic energy.

What’s extraordinary about Tempest is how it balances comedy, sensuality, regret, fantasy, and intellectual ambition without becoming pretentious. The film feels like a midlife crisis rewritten as a Mediterranean myth. Mazursky understood that reinvention is never clean; even escape carries your old self with it.

There’s also a generosity in the movie’s worldview that feels increasingly rare. Mazursky doesn’t mock yearning. He believes people genuinely want transformation, even if they bungle it repeatedly. That compassion runs through nearly all his work.

He belongs to that generation of American directors who made films for adults without making them cold — emotionally literate cinema that trusted conversation, ambiguity, and performance more than plot mechanics. Watching Mazursky now, you realize how unusual it is for movies to allow characters to simply be complicated.
  - I share your love for **Tempest** \- a much neglected and unappreciated gem of a film.  Also, for what it is worth, on a long ago trip to NYC he sat right next to me while having a breakfast at a swanky hotel.  I recognized him from *Curb*, but I let him dine in peace.
  - Thanks for a really thoughtful response.

Any thoughts on why he hasn't been rediscovered by younger audiences? 
  - I really like Blume in Love but rarely see it mentioned when Mazursky’s work is discussed. Would be curious how you feel about that one. Like a lot of his earlier work, it is very specific to its time and place, and there is one scene in particular that probably wouldn’t land well with a modern audience. Yet, I still rate it among his best. 
2. Reposting to meet length requirements:

I worked on his last feature film, shot in Toronto with Richard Dreyfuss and Judi Davis. He was very laid back and funny, knew what he wanted. And open to collaboration from all. It was a pretty fun one to work on.  

He was a good actor too, memorable in the Sopranos.  
3. I'm happy to take this opportunity to praise Winchell. It went straight to video, I remember. But I thought it was a quite good biopic about a complicated person. So many biopics want to make one single point about someone's life--as if they have a thesis statement about the person and won't deviate from it. Winchell wasn't like that. It also had one of the sexiest sex scenes in my memory.
4. Never heard of him, but I just wanted to thank you for reminding me of one of my favorite movie scenes:

From Alpha Dog (2006)

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d7ufrSM1IgY](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d7ufrSM1IgY)

"If anybody sees Johnny Truelove, tell them JAKE MAZURSKY, is looking for him.  Thank you."

## 5. Pixar Should Start Planning 40 Year Reissues - Preserving the Canon

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Pixar's first 10 films are amongst the greatest late 20th and early 21st century American films, particularly for children/animated works. They aren't necessarily considered amongst the all-time great films by critics, but there are two entries on the extended Sight & Sound list (Toy Story #629 & Wall-E #907), and all received the best Animated Feature Academy Award once that was introduced (except Cars).

When I recently revisited A Bug's Life, I thought about the declining legacy of these early films. The technology was so new that the visual palette is simple and rough-edged, to the point where the look distracts from the core ambition of the characters and story. This isn't like classic Disney films where the animators created a timeless visual style. These early Pixar films simply look dated compared to anything released since 2015, give or take.

Disney is no stranger to re-releases and money grabs, so I worry that they would screw this up. But I think Pixar should seriously consider high-profile re-releases of these first 10 films. These would separate films (e.g., 'The Incredibles - 40th Edition'), and the original films would still exist as stand-alone titles. The focus would be simply to "correct" the visual look based on technological progress over the last couple decades -- added texture, background depth, and other visual fixes. However, the creative teams could also do some work making minor changes to character designs or locations without risk to the original films (I'm thinking Sid from Toy Story 1). Hopefully the original film's vision would stay intact and this wouldn't be a George Lucas situation.

This would mean a re-release of Toy Story in 1935 and subsequent features every two years or so (Bug's Life, Toy Story 2, Monster's Inc, Finding Nemo, Incredibles, Cars, Ratatouille, Wall-E, Up). If done right, these films would be more approachable to future generations of film watchers, and would better cement the legacy of these films even if the different versions could complicate the viewing experience.

Anyone else struggle to watch these early Pixar films based only on the outdated visual style?

Extended Sight & Sound list: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/15NQFoApM3yKAnho40Z0r4BKPVZIKCf1zgOsPR0ClA10/edit?usp=drivesdk

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I guess this is one of those things where I don’t have a super passionate opinion where I read your post and start fuming, but I don’t know. I think there’s something magical about a movie reflecting the time and technology that produced it. I guess I would never, you know, deny you watching a movie how you want, but I honestly can’t think of a movie that I would prefer to watch some updated version you describe over the original.
  - I'd never thought this was something I wanted either, for example I like outdated CGI as a hallmark of the time (usually in popcorn fare like the 90's Anaconda). Something about these Pixar movies being actually important and meaningful works make me think they could benefit from a re-issue. Like restoring a piece of classical art, except here the goal would be to enhance rather than restore the original vision. Perhaps an impossible goal.
    - You know I’m not sure I entirely disagree with you, so long as of course the original version was an available, I think you’re right in that a reissue might  be worthwhile. I keep trying to think of a reason why not (besides the possibility that the original was made unavailable) but I really can’t, other than some moralism on the sanctity of art or whatever. The analogy to restoring a person classical work is compelling. 
      - I bet there are people over at Pixar that would rather do this than make Toy Story 5. Thankfully, the studio still has a lot of leeway to make original films (Hoppers, etc.), but they are still cranking out sequels to pay the bills. This would be a return to the (indie) studio movie work that started it all.
2. This would not go over well. Special editions that tinker with beloved classics are more wildly unpopular today than they have ever been. 

Also, for better or worse, the average person does not understand or care much about visual presentation quality like many hardcore film enthusiasts. Just look at the 🧠💀 top comments under literally any restoration comparison that gets posted online. 

Millennials in particular have come to associate antiquated visuals with nostalgia, which is their #1 thing.
  - I think people would make an exception for early CGI.  The first Toy Story is extremely primitive and it looks so much worse in actuality than it does in your head.  If it were redone to look like you remember, I doubt anyone would be upset.  What you want is a remaster that doesn't look redone at all until you compare the original and remaster side-by-side.

Worth noting that the same is true of video games: they get criticized when they deviate from the source but are celebrated when they make the games feel like they always should have looked that way.  For instance, Christian Whitehead's Sonic the Hedgehog remasters are hugely popular.
    - lol you are deeply underestimating millennial’s hostility and protectiveness surrounding their sacred childhood movies.

Given that Disney already spent huge $$$ to completely re-render Toy Story in 2K for blu-ray (later upscaled and given an HDR pass for 4K UHD) and also 3D conversion, I can’t see them tinkering with it again. 

Especially with Disney+ killing the appetite for theatrical reissues in general. The cow is officially milked. 

  - I agree that this would probably go over poorly, given the corporate financial interests. I'm thinking of The Wizard of Oz at the Sphere (haven't seen it) that has generally been critically derided. My case is more that this would be valuable as a carefully-planned creative endeavor, and would still keep the O.G. films intact.
    - Sphere’s AI “enhanced” Wizard of Oz is an immersive experience, which is different than simply updating its visuals to be in line with modern production values. 

Personally, despite Oz being my all-time favorite movie, I refuse to financially support that slop. Outside of professional/traditional restoration efforts classic films should be left alone.
